Reformation of Abuses at Rome by the Emperor Theodosius.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Reformation of Abuses at Rome by the Emperor Theodosius.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The emperor Theodosius during his short stay in Italy, conferred the greatest benefit on the city of Rome, by grants on the one hand, and abrogations on the other.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: His largesses were indeed very munificent; and he removed two most infamous abuses which existed in the city.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: One of them was the following: there were buildings of immense magnitude, erected in ancient Rome in former times, in which bread was made for distribution among the people.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Those who had the charge of these edifices, who Mancipes were called in the Latin language, in process of time converted them into receptacles for thieves.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Now as the bake-houses in these structures were placed underneath, they build taverns at the side of each, where they kept prostitutes; by which means they entrapped many of those who went thither either for the sake of refreshment, or to gratify their lusts, for by a certain mechanical contrivance they precipitated them from the tavern into the bake-house below.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This was practiced chiefly upon strangers; and such as were in this way kidnapped were compelled to work in the bake-houses, where many of them were immured until old age, not being allowed to go out, and giving the impression to their friends that they were dead.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: It happened that one of the soldiers of the emperor Theodosius fell into this snare; who being shut up in the bake-house, and hindered from going out, drew a dagger which he wore and killed those who stood in his way: the rest being terrified, suffered him to escape.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: When the emperor was made acquainted with the circumstance he punished the Mancipes, and ordered these haunts of lawless and abandoned characters to be pulled down.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This was one of the disgraceful nuisances of which the emperor purged the imperial city: the other was of this nature.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: When a woman was detected in adultery, they punished the delinquent not in the way of correction but rather of aggravation of her crime.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For shutting her up in a narrow brothel, they obliged her to prostitute herself in a most disgusting manner; causing little bells to be rung at the time of the unclean deed that those who passed might not be ignorant of what was doing within.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This was doubtless intended to brand the crime with greater ignominy in public opinion.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: As soon as the emperor was apprised of this indecent usage, he would by no means tolerate it; but having ordered the Sistra —for so these places of penal prostitution were denominated—to be pulled down, he appointed other laws for the punishment of adulteresses.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Thus did the emperor Theodosius free the city from two of its most discreditable abuses: and when he had arranged all other affairs to his satisfaction, he left the emperor Valentinian at Rome, and returned himself with his son Honorius to Constantinople, and entered that city of the 10th of November, in the consulate of Tatian and Symmachus.
